definition: Cooling that pipes liquid to cold plates on hot components (CPU/GPU) instead of relying on air. Increasingly mandatory as accelerator TDPs exceed what air can handle. HPE ships fanless 100% DLC for top systems.
Submerging servers in a dielectric fluid (single- or two-phase) to remove heat. Enables extreme density but requires new mechanical/facility design.
The unit that circulates and controls coolant between the facility water loop and the server/rack liquid-cooling loop — the heart of a DLC deployment.
A liquid-cooled radiator mounted on the rack's back door that cools exhaust air, letting existing air-cooled racks handle higher density without full DLC.
Power drawn per rack. Traditional racks were 5-15 kW; AI racks (e.g., NVL72) now hit 100-140+ kW, forcing liquid cooling and data-center power/thermal redesign.
The heat (in watts) a cooling solution must dissipate for a chip at sustained load. Server CPU/GPU TDPs have climbed past 500W-1kW+, driving the shift to liquid cooling.
Ratio of total facility power to IT-equipment power. 1.0 is ideal; lower is more efficient. Liquid cooling and better design push PUE down, cutting energy cost and carbon.
Higher-voltage (48V vs 12V) rack/board power distribution that reduces current and resistive losses — increasingly standard (via OCP) for high-power AI racks.
